title: VPN Kill Switch Explained: What It Is and Why You Need It
description: A kill switch cuts your internet if the VPN drops, preventing your real IP from leaking. Here is how it works and which VPNs implement it correctly.
content:
What a Kill Switch Does

A VPN kill switch monitors your VPN connection continuously. The moment the VPN tunnel drops, whether from a server issue, network change, or app crash, the kill switch blocks all outbound internet traffic. Your real IP never reaches the destination server.

Without a kill switch, a brief VPN disconnection exposes your actual IP address for seconds or minutes before you notice. For most casual users this is an inconvenience. For journalists, activists, or people on public WiFi handling sensitive data, it is a serious exposure.

System-Level vs App-Level Kill Switches

System-level kill switches operate at the operating system firewall layer. They block all traffic from every application when the VPN drops. This is the most reliable implementation because it does not depend on the VPN app remaining open.

App-level kill switches only block traffic from the VPN application itself. If you have another browser or torrent client open, those continue unblocked after a VPN drop. Less effective for users who want total protection.

Split Tunneling and Kill Switches

Split tunneling lets you route some apps through the VPN and others through your regular connection. When used alongside a kill switch, you need to clarify which traffic the kill switch covers. Some VPNs apply the kill switch only to VPN-tunneled traffic, leaving split-tunnel exclusions unaffected. Read the documentation for your specific provider.

Which VPNs Have the Best Kill Switches

Mullvad has the most reliable system-level kill switch. It activates on first connection and persists across reboots. Even if you uninstall Mullvad without manually disabling the kill switch, the firewall rules remain active.

ProtonVPN offers both a standard kill switch and an always-on kill switch that blocks traffic before the VPN connects, not just after it drops.

NordVPN implements a solid system-level kill switch on Windows, macOS, and Linux. The mobile versions use app-level protection only.

ExpressVPN calls their implementation Network Lock. It works reliably on desktop but has had inconsistencies on macOS after system sleep cycles according to user reports in 2025.

How to Test Your Kill Switch

Connect to your VPN. Open a browser and visit an IP-checker site. Note your VPN IP. Then simulate a VPN drop by going into your network adapter settings and disabling the adapter briefly. Check whether the IP-checker loads during the disconnection. If it loads with your real IP, the kill switch failed.
